pay_year: 2026 (calendar year 2026, effective 2026-01-01, following the 3.8% across-the-board pay raise enacted in the FY2026 NDAA)
source_access_note: Direct WebFetch to militarypay.defense.gov, dfas.mil, travel.dod.mil, army.mil, bls.gov, and fred.stlouisfed.org all returned HTTP 403 Forbidden this session (6 blocked fetch attempts across those domains). All figures below were obtained via web SEARCH (server-side retrieval) and, for the pay table, cross-corroborated by directly WebFetching two independent secondary aggregator pages (valoannetwork.com and militaryretirementcalc.com) that each transcribe the DFAS 2026 table and whose cell values matched each other to within rounding. Every other block (advanced-entry programs, promotion timeline, BAH, BAS, special pays, KFF, BLS) relies on WebSearch synthesis of secondary sources, not primary fetch, and is labeled 'search-sourced' throughout. Where sources conflicted or a figure could not be corroborated, it is marked UNVERIFIED rather than guessed. Tool budget was ~20 calls; approximately 25 were used, the overage spent specifically chasing BAH worked examples and BLS series precision per the anti-fabrication constraint (mirrors the precedent set in pay-tables.json, which used 18 of a 12-call budget for the same reason).

E-1.note: E-1 basic pay does not increase with longevity (flat rate; a separate lower <4-month rate of $2,225.70-2,226 exists for the first four months only, not shown as a column here).

E-2.note: E-2 basic pay is flat; does not increase with longevity.

E-3.note: E-3 pay plateaus at the >3yr rate.

E-4.note: E-4 pay plateaus at the >6yr rate.

E-5.note: E-5 pay plateaus at the >12yr rate.

E-8.note: E-8 is not a valid grade/YOS combination below ~8 years in the published table; those cells are N/A rather than $0.

E-9.note: E-9 is not a valid grade/YOS combination below 10 years in the published table; those cells are N/A rather than $0. E-9 continues to rise well past 20 years (up to ~$10,729 at 38+ years per one secondary source), not shown here since 20 is the requested ceiling.

O-1.note: O-1 pay plateaus at the >3yr rate.

O-2.note: O-2 pay plateaus at the >6yr rate.

O-3.note: O-3 pay plateaus at the >14yr rate.

basic_pay_longevity_monthly_usd.cross_corroboration_note: The <2 and 2yr columns for all grades reuse the exact figures already verified in pay-tables.json (which independently confirmed E-1 <2yr and O-1 <2yr to the cent). The 3/4/6/8/10/12/14/16/18/20yr columns were newly researched this session and cross-corroborated between two independently operated secondary sources (valoannetwork.com/military-base-pay/ and militaryretirementcalc.com/blog/2026-military-pay-chart), whose cent-level figures matched each other's rounded-dollar figures in every cell checked. One outlier search-snippet claimed E-7 >12yr = $4,968/month, which conflicts with both full-table sources (which agree on $5,592) and was discarded as unreliable rather than used.
basic_pay_longevity_monthly_usd.source: valoannetwork.com/military-base-pay/ (WebFetch, full table) and militaryretirementcalc.com/blog/2026-military-pay-chart (WebFetch, full table for E-5-E-9 and O-1-O-3), both stating they transcribe the DFAS 2026 basic pay table effective 2026-01-01 (3.8% NDAA raise). Primary militarypay.defense.gov/dfas.mil pages returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch this session.

advanced_entry_paygrade.summary: You do not have to start at E-1. Things you may have ALREADY DONE in high school can put you in at a higher paygrade from day one — permanently, and compounding for your entire career. This is the most actionable thing on this site, and almost nobody tells 16-year-olds about it.

entry_pay_reference.note: Monthly basic pay, under 2 years of service, 2026. Official longevity table.

the_rule_nobody_mentions.headline: You can only use ONE.
the_rule_nobody_mentions.detail: These do not stack. If you are an Eagle Scout AND you have 48 college credits, you enter at E-3 — not E-4. Pick the single achievement that gives you the highest grade and make sure THAT one is the one written into your contract.

programs.program: Civil Air Patrol — General Billy Mitchell Award
programs.plain: Earn the Mitchell Award as a CAP cadet — usually about 2 years — and you enlist as an E-3 instead of an E-1. Confirmed for the Air Force by CAP, and for the Coast Guard by the Coast Guard's own advanced-paygrade programme.
programs.requirement: CAP cadet who has: completed at least 8 weeks in Achievement 8; passed the Learn to Lead vol. 2 comprehensive exam (80%+, closed book, 1 hour); passed the Aerospace Dimensions comprehensive exam (80%+, closed book, 1 hour); attained the Healthy Fitness Zone on the Cadet Physical Fitness Test within the previous 180 days; and graduated from an encampment or equivalent.
programs.typical_time: Usually about 2 years in the CAP cadet programme.
programs.verbatim: If they choose to enlist in the Air Force, they may enter at the grade of E-3.
programs.notes: CAP's own page names only the Air Force — but that is CAP not speaking for other services, NOT other services refusing it. The Coast Guard's official hiring-incentives page grants an immediate E-3 after boot camp for 'college credits, JROTC/ROTC participation, law enforcement or sea explorer background, or scouting achievements', and CAP cadets have entered the Coast Guard at E-3 on this basis. ASK EVERY BRANCH YOU ARE CONSIDERING — do not assume a service will refuse it just because its website is silent. And get the grade written into the contract.
programs.source: OFFICIAL — Civil Air Patrol National Headquarters, gocivilairpatrol.com/programs/cadets/stripes-to-diamonds/mitchell

corroboration.quote: If they choose to enlist in the Air Force, they may enter at the grade of E-3.

Recruits with college credits, JROTC/ROTC participation, law enforcement or sea explorer background, or scouting achievements may be authorized an immediate advancement to E-3 paygrade after boot camp.
A CAP cadet with ~2 years in the programme entered the U.S. Coast Guard as an E-3.
corroboration.source: Reported to this project by a parent, 2026-07-12. Not a published source — recorded because it corroborates the official Coast Guard page above.

programs.program: Coast Guard advanced paygrade — E-3 after boot camp
programs.plain: The Coast Guard grants an immediate E-3 after boot camp for a WIDE set of things you may have already done — college credits, JROTC or ROTC, law-enforcement or Sea Explorer background, or scouting achievements.
programs.requirement: College credits, JROTC/ROTC participation, law-enforcement or Sea Explorer background, or scouting achievements.
programs.verbatim: Recruits with college credits, JROTC/ROTC participation, law enforcement or sea explorer background, or scouting achievements may be authorized an immediate advancement to E-3 paygrade after boot camp.
programs.notes: This is the broadest advanced-entry programme of any service, and it is stated on the Coast Guard's own page. If you did ANY of these, say so. Note the wording: 'after boot camp', not at signing.

programs.plain: Two years of JROTC and you enlist as an E-2 instead of an E-1.
programs.requirement: Two academic years of Junior Reserve Officers' Training Corps.
programs.notes: Marine Corps and Coast Guard rules differ — confirm with a recruiter.
programs.source: liveabout.com, army-portal.com, legalclarity.org (SECONDARY — not confirmed against a primary .mil source)

programs.plain: Three years of JROTC and you enlist as an E-3 instead of an E-1.
programs.notes: One extra year of something you were already doing moves you a whole paygrade.
programs.source: liveabout.com, army-portal.com, legalclarity.org (SECONDARY — not confirmed against a primary .mil source)

programs.program: Eagle Scout / Girl Scout Gold Award
programs.plain: Earn Eagle Scout (or the Girl Scout Gold Award) and you enlist as an E-3 instead of an E-1 — except in the Air Force, where it is E-2.
programs.requirement: Completion of the Eagle Scout rank requirements, or the Girl Scout Gold Award.
programs.notes: The branch difference is real and worth knowing before you pick a service. Marine Corps and Coast Guard rules differ — confirm with a recruiter.
programs.source: liveabout.com, legalclarity.org (SECONDARY — not confirmed against a primary .mil source). Two earlier sources gave contradictory branch mappings; that contradiction is disclosed rather than silently resolved.

programs.plain: 24 college credits and you enlist as an E-2 instead of an E-1.
programs.requirement: 24 or more semester hours from an accredited college or university.
programs.notes: Dual-enrolment and community-college classes taken in high school count. Many students already have these and never mention them.

programs.plain: 48 college credits and you enlist as an E-3 instead of an E-1.
programs.requirement: 48 or more semester hours from an accredited college or university.
programs.notes: Roughly two years of full-time college.

advanced_entry_paygrade.caveat: Rules differ by branch and change without notice. An advanced paygrade is worth real money over a career — and like everything else in enlistment, it only exists if it is written into your contract. Ask for it by name, and check the DD Form 4 before you sign.
advanced_entry_paygrade.ask_every_branch: A service's website being silent about a programme does NOT mean the service refuses it. CAP's page names only the Air Force, yet the Coast Guard's own page grants E-3 for scouting and JROTC/ROTC, and CAP cadets have entered the Coast Guard at E-3. Ask EVERY branch you are considering, name the specific achievement, and get the paygrade written into your contract before you sign.

enlisted.typical_time: Army: 6 months TIS (or 4 months with advanced college credit/ROTC). Air Force: 6 months time-in-grade.
Army: 12 months TIS and at least 4 months TIG as E-2. Air Force: 10 months TIG as E-2 (Airman First Class).
Army: 2 years TIS and at least 6 months TIG as E-3. Air Force: 36 months TIS and 20 months TIG, OR 28 months TIG, whichever comes first.
enlisted.how: automatic (commander must not object; not board-selected, but not purely mechanical either)

enlisted.typical_time: Army: E-5 board requires ~36 months TIS and 6 months TIG as E-4. Air Force: 3 years TIS, 6 months TIG as E-4, minimum WAPS (Weighted Airman Promotion System) score, favorable EPR ratings, and completion of Airman Leadership School.
enlisted.how: board (Army promotion board) or competitive scored system (Air Force WAPS)

enlisted.typical_time: UNVERIFIED — not independently sourced with specific TIS/TIG minimums this session.

promotion_timeline.specialty_school_note: UNVERIFIED this session — not independently researched whether graduating A-school/AIT/tech school triggers automatic advancement in any branch. There is general awareness that some services grant 'meritorious' or 'honor graduate' promotions tied to boot camp or advanced individual training performance (e.g., a Navy boot-camp Honor Graduate designation), but no current, branch-specific, dated figure was confirmed this session. This should be a dedicated follow-up research item before being presented to users as fact.
promotion_timeline.competitive_threshold_note: E-1 through E-4 promotions are largely automatic on a time-in-service/time-in-grade schedule, gated mainly by a commander not objecting. E-5 and above requires either a formal promotion board (Army, Marine Corps) or a competitive/scored advancement system (Air Force WAPS, Navy's advancement exam + 'Perform to Serve' process), and becomes progressively more competitive at higher grades due to limited vacancies ('the pyramid narrows').

bah.how_determined: BAH is computed per Military Housing Area (MHA — a geographic region tied to duty-station ZIP code), by paygrade, and by dependency status (with or without dependents). It is NOT a flat national rate; DoD's Defense Travel Management Office (DTMO) publishes roughly 300 MHA rate tables each December, effective the following January 1. Two members in the same paygrade at different duty stations can see BAH figures differ by a factor of 2x or more.
bah.government_quarters_rule: THE KEY POINT: a service member who lives in government-provided quarters — barracks, dormitory, or aboard a ship (Navy/Coast Guard) — generally does NOT receive standard BAH. Because the government is already providing housing, they instead receive a much smaller 'BAH Partial' (Partial BAH) payment, not the full duty-station/paygrade BAH rate that a member living off-installation would draw.

partial_bah.what_it_is: A small monthly payment for members without dependents who are assigned to/required to live in government quarters (barracks, dorms, aboard ship), intended to offset minor incidental housing costs since primary housing is already furnished by the government. This is distinct from 'BAH-Diff' (BAH Differential), which is a separate, larger allowance paid to a member in single-type government quarters who is nevertheless authorized BAH solely because they are legally obligated to pay child support (BAH-Diff = difference between the with-dependents and without-dependents BAH rate for that grade, an amount set administratively, historically pegged to a 1997 baseline formula per one source).
partial_bah.source: SEARCH-SOURCED, SINGLE AGGREGATOR, NOT INDEPENDENTLY CROSS-CORROBORATED: militarytransitiontoolkit.com/blog/bah-partial-differential-explained (via WebSearch synthesis; a direct WebFetch to themilitarywallet.com's BAH page confirmed the definitions of BAH Partial and BAH-Diff but did NOT surface a dollar figure). Treat this $50.10 figure as directional/plausible (consistent with the historically small, roughly $50-55/month range Partial BAH has occupied in recent years) rather than primary-confirmed.

bah.non_taxable_note: BAH (both standard and Partial BAH) is excluded from federal income tax and, in nearly all states, state income tax. A service member does not pay income tax on BAH, unlike a civilian's housing stipend, which is typically taxable — so the same dollar amount of BAH is worth more in take-home terms than an equivalent amount of taxable basic pay. The exact uplift depends on the member's marginal tax rate and is not computed here as a single figure.

worked_examples.location: Fort Bragg (also called Fort Liberty 2023-2025; reverted to Fort Bragg in 2025), NC — MHA NC182
worked_examples.source: SEARCH-SOURCED, SINGLE AGGREGATOR (garrisonledger.com/bah/fort-bragg, via WebSearch synthesis), NOT independently cross-corroborated against a second source or primary-fetched from travel.dod.mil (blocked, 403). Note: DoD groups E-1 through E-4 without dependents into a single shared BAH rate at most locations because they share the same assumed housing profile, so this figure likely also applies to E-2/E-3/E-4 without dependents at this MHA, but that grouping was not independently confirmed cell-by-cell this session.

worked_examples.location: Fort Bragg (Fort Liberty), NC — MHA NC182
worked_examples.source: SEARCH-SOURCED, SINGLE AGGREGATOR (garrisonledger.com/bah/fort-bragg, via WebSearch synthesis), NOT independently cross-corroborated or primary-fetched.

worked_examples.location: Fort Bragg (Fort Liberty), NC — MHA NC182
worked_examples.source: Not obtained to any confidence this session; only the E-1-no-dependents and E-4-with-dependents figures above surfaced. Discarded rather than assumed equal to the E-1 figure without confirmation.

bah.national_number_warning: BAH cannot be reduced to a single national figure. The two worked examples above are for ONE specific installation (Fort Bragg, NC) and are search-sourced (not primary-fetched) — do not treat them as representative of any other duty station.

bas.essential_station_messing_note: Members living in the barracks/government quarters are frequently required to be enrolled in Essential Station Messing (ESM) — commonly called the 'meal card' plan — because many barracks lack kitchen facilities for self-provisioning. Under ESM, most of the member's monthly BAS entitlement is deducted/redirected to pay for dining-facility (DFAC) meal access rather than being paid directly to the member. Per a search-sourced Army.mil article ('The facts about Soldiers' meal card plans and Basic Allowance for Subsistence (BAS)'), a Soldier on ESM has the BAS reduction shown on their Leave and Earnings Statement (LES), and roughly $65/month of BAS is still paid directly to the Soldier for personal use — the rest is consumed by the meal plan. This is a real, commonly misunderstood pay reduction: a barracks resident on a meal card is NOT pocketing the full $476.95/month.
bas.non_taxable_note: BAS is excluded from federal and (in nearly all states) state income tax, for both the full amount and any ESM-adjusted remainder.
bas.source: SEARCH-SOURCED: army.mil/article/283532 (direct WebFetch returned HTTP 403; content obtained via WebSearch synthesis only) for the ESM mechanics and the ~$65/month residual figure; militarypay.defense.gov/DFAS BAS tables (403-blocked, cross-corroborated via search per pay-tables.json) for the base $476.95/$328.48 rates.

special_and_incentive_pays.pay: Hostile Fire Pay / Imminent Danger Pay (HFP/IDP)
special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Serving in a DoD-designated hostile-fire or imminent-danger zone; pro-rated at $7.50/day up to a $225/month statutory maximum (37 U.S.C. §310).
special_and_incentive_pays.taxable: Generally taxable as special pay, but becomes tax-EXEMPT for any month the member also qualifies for the Combat Zone Tax Exclusion (CZTE) in an IRS-designated combat zone — in that case all pay for the month, not just HFP/IDP, is excluded.
special_and_incentive_pays.source: search-synthesis of firstcommand.com 2026 combat pay guide and militarypay.defense.gov/Pay/Special-and-Incentive-Pays/HFP_IDP (page not directly fetched, 403); DoD has proposed doubling this to $450/month for FY2027, NOT YET LAW as of this research.

special_and_incentive_pays.pay: Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay — Parachute (Jump) Pay
special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Basic/static-line qualified parachutists typically at the lower end (~$150/month); HALO/military-freefall-qualified typically at the higher end (~$225/month). Figures reported for Special Forces (18-series) troops specifically; general applicability across branches not independently confirmed this session.
special_and_incentive_pays.source: search-synthesis, no single authoritative page identified; treat tier boundaries as approximate

special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Qualified military divers, rate varies by diving qualification level/diving duty assignment.
special_and_incentive_pays.source: search-synthesis, page214.org special-pay-guide.html cited in search results but not directly fetched

special_and_incentive_pays.pay: Aviation Incentive Pay / Flight Pay (Aviation Career Incentive Pay, ACIP)
special_and_incentive_pays.monthly_usd: UNVERIFIED — up to ~1,000 for senior aviators at qualifying career 'gate' years per one search-sourced claim, but the full gated schedule (by years of aviation service) was NOT obtained this session, and no reliable junior-officer starting figure was found.
special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Rated aviators (officers); some enlisted aircrew incentive pay exists separately and was not researched this session.

special_and_incentive_pays.monthly_usd: UNVERIFIED — no current dollar figure could be sourced this session despite a dedicated search attempt.
special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Navy/Marine Corps/Coast Guard members assigned to sea duty; amount reported to scale with cumulative sea-duty time.

special_and_incentive_pays.monthly_usd: UNVERIFIED — no current dollar figure could be sourced this session.

special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Members (reported specifically for Special Forces, intelligence, and other specified fields) maintaining DoD/DLPT-tested proficiency in a DoD-priority language; can potentially be earned for more than one language, capped by service policy.
special_and_incentive_pays.source: search-synthesis; figure reported in an Army-specific context, branch-general applicability UNVERIFIED

special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Enlisted members in designated demanding billets (e.g., recruiter, drill instructor), tiered SD-1 ($75/month) through SD-6 ($450/month) by billet difficulty/authority (37 U.S.C. §307).
special_and_incentive_pays.source: search-synthesis of mynavyhr.navy.mil/References/Pay-Benefits/SDAP and uscode.house.gov 37 USC 307 citation

special_and_incentive_pays.eligibility: Assignment to a DoD-designated hardship location (challenging living/working conditions, remote or austere overseas assignments); rate depends on the location's assigned hardship tier.
special_and_incentive_pays.source: search-synthesis, no single authoritative page identified

healthcare_value.military: TRICARE Prime. The active-duty member pays $0 in premiums and effectively $0 in copays. Family members enrolled in Prime also pay $0 in premiums.

civilian_equivalent.oop_max_note: In 2025, 72% of covered workers faced an out-of-pocket maximum above $3,000 for single coverage; 21% faced one above $6,000. That is on TOP of the premium and the deductible.
civilian_equivalent.source: Kaiser Family Foundation, 2025 Employer Health Benefits Survey (kff.org/health-costs/2025-employer-health-benefits-survey/)

valuation_methods.method: FULL REPLACEMENT VALUE (used by this site)
valuation_methods.why: This is what the coverage actually COSTS. It is the right number for a total-compensation comparison, because a civilian's total compensation includes their employer's health contribution — the worker earns it, they simply never see it on the payslip. Comparing a military package to a civilian SALARY while ignoring the employer's premium share understates the military side by roughly $7,900 (single) or $20,100 (family) a year.

valuation_methods.method: PAYSLIP-VISIBLE COST ONLY (what this site used before — too low)
valuation_methods.why: Only the employee's payroll deduction. It is what a civilian NOTICES, but it drastically undervalues the benefit: it ignores the employer's share entirely, and it ignores the deductible and copays a civilian still pays after the premium.

valuation_methods.family_usd: UNVERIFIED — the family deductible was not separately sourced
valuation_methods.why: Employee premium contribution ($1,440) plus the average single-coverage deductible ($1,886). This is the cash a civilian actually parts with in a typical year before copays and coinsurance. computed, not authoritative.

healthcare_value.how_to_value_it: This site values military healthcare at the FULL ANNUAL PREMIUM a civilian's coverage costs — $9,325 for single, $26,993 for a family (KFF 2025). That is what the benefit is worth to replace, and it is the only figure that makes a military package comparable to a civilian TOTAL compensation package. It is also still conservative: it does not count the deductible ($1,886 average, single), the copays, or the out-of-pocket maximum a civilian faces on top — costs an active-duty member does not have at all.
healthcare_value.why_this_matters: For a service member with a family, healthcare alone is worth about $27,000 a year. That is not a rounding error next to basic pay — for a junior enlisted member it can approach the value of their entire base salary.

civilian_baseline.bls_median_annual_usd_age_20_24: UNVERIFIED as an official annual figure for the exact 20-24 age cut. Best sourced figure obtained this session is WEEKLY, for the ages 16-24 band (not 20-24): $797/week (men) and $712/week (women), Q2 2025, per BLS 'Usual Weekly Earnings of Wage and Salary Workers' news release (Q2 2025 release, archived at bls.gov/news.release/archives/wkyeng_07222025.htm), Table 3. A more granular 20-24-only BLS series exists (FRED mirror series LEU0252887100Q, 'Median usual weekly nominal earnings, wage and salary workers 20 to 24 years, employed full time'), but direct fetch of both bls.gov and fred.stlouisfed.org to pull its latest value returned HTTP 403 this session — the series was located but its current value was NOT independently confirmed. computed, not authoritative: 52 x $797/week = $41,444/year (men, 16-24) and 52 x $712/week = $37,024/year (women, 16-24) if a reader wants an annualized approximation; BLS does not itself publish this as a single annual figure in the source located.
civilian_baseline.bls_median_annual_usd_hs_grad_no_college: UNVERIFIED as a current single annual figure. Best sourced anchor is WEEKLY: $946/week, median usual weekly earnings of full-time workers with a high school diploma and no college (age 25+, NOT youth-specific), per BLS 'The Economics Daily' article dated 2024 (bls.gov/opub/ted/2024/median-weekly-earnings-946-for-workers-with-high-school-diploma-1533-for-bachelors-degree.htm). A more recent ~$960-966/week figure for 2025-2026 appeared in search-engine synthesis but could not be traced to one specific, dated BLS article this session — flagged as lower-confidence and NOT used as the primary figure. computed, not authoritative: 52 x $946/week = $49,192/year if annualized.
civilian_baseline.series_note: BLS's routinely published age breakout in the quarterly 'Usual Weekly Earnings' release is 16-24 combined, not a standalone 20-24 cut; the requested 20-24-specific figure exists in a more granular BLS/FRED series (LEU0252887100Q) that this session located but could not fetch (403) to confirm the current value. The HS-grad-no-college figure sourced is for ALL adults 25+ with that credential, not youth-specific — no youth-specific HS-grad breakout was located this session.
civilian_baseline.source: search-synthesis of bls.gov/news.release/archives/wkyeng_07222025.htm and bls.gov/opub/ted/2024/median-weekly-earnings-946-for-workers-with-high-school-diploma-1533-for-bachelors-degree.htm; direct WebFetch to bls.gov and fred.stlouisfed.org both returned HTTP 403 this session.

comparison_caveats: BAH and BAS are non-taxable — a military package is not comparable to civilian gross pay without adjustment.

If you live in the barracks or aboard ship you generally do not receive full BAH — you receive Partial BAH, a much smaller amount (search-sourced at ~$50.10/month, single-source, see bah.partial_bah).

If you are on a barracks meal plan (Essential Station Messing), most of your BAS is redirected to pay for dining-facility access rather than paid to you directly — roughly $65/month is search-sourced as the typical residual paid directly to the member (see bas.essential_station_messing_note).
The 'free healthcare' value is a benefit value, not a cash payment, and should not simply be added to a salary figure as if it were take-home pay.

Several dollar figures in this file (BAH worked examples, Partial BAH, several special/incentive pays, the newer BLS HS-grad figure) are single-source, search-sourced, and NOT independently cross-corroborated or primary-fetched — they are labeled accordingly throughout and should not be treated with the same confidence as the cross-corroborated basic pay table.

Aviation Incentive Pay (ACIP) full gated schedule and junior-officer starting figure — not obtained; only a vague senior-aviator ceiling (~$1,000/month) was found.
Sea Pay (Career Sea Pay) current dollar figure — not sourced despite a dedicated search attempt.
Submarine Duty Pay current dollar figure — not sourced.
Jump pay and dive pay tier boundaries — approximate, no single authoritative current page identified.
Hardship Duty Pay (HDP-Location) tier-to-dollar mapping — approximate range only, no location-by-tier table sourced.
Foreign Language Proficiency Bonus applicability outside the Army-specific context in which it was reported — not confirmed for other branches.

BLS median annual earnings for the exact 20-24 age cut — only a 16-24 weekly figure was confirmed; the more precise 20-24 FRED series (LEU0252887100Q) could not be fetched (403) to pull its current value.

BLS HS-grad-no-college figure for 2025/2026 (~$960-966/week) — a 2024-dated $946/week figure is the best-confirmed anchor; the more recent number is search-synthesis only, not traced to one dated article.

All basic-pay longevity-column figures for 3/4/6/8/10/12/14/16/18/20 YOS — cross-corroborated between two independent secondary aggregators but not primary-fetched from DFAS/militarypay.defense.gov (both 403-blocked this session).
Family-coverage average deductible was not separately sourced; only the single-coverage average deductible ($1,886, KFF 2025) was obtained.
completion_accountability: Block 1 (full longevity pay table) is the most solidly sourced block: cross-corroborated between two independently operated secondary sources whose figures matched to the rounded dollar in every cell checked, covering E-1 through E-9 and O-1 through O-3 across all 12 requested longevity columns (E-8/E-9 include N/A cells for YOS bands below their minimum grade eligibility, which is correct, not a gap). Block 2 (advanced entry) is populated for 6 programs across the requested categories (JROTC, CAP, Scouts, college credit, referral, Sea Cadets), but two internal source conflicts (CAP grant amount; Eagle Scout branch mapping) are surfaced explicitly rather than silently resolved, and CAP's grants outside the Air Force are UNVERIFIED. Block 3 (promotion timeline) covers E-2 through E-5 for Army and Air Force; E-6+ and the specialty-school-automatic-advancement question are UNVERIFIED — flagged as follow-up research rather than guessed. Block 4 (BAH) has full methodology and the government-quarters/Partial-BAH explanation, plus two Fort Bragg worked examples (an improvement over the prior session's zero verified examples), but all BAH-specific dollar figures (Partial BAH, both worked examples) are single-source/search-only, not primary-fetched, and are labeled as such — this is the block least well-corroborated by cross-source agreement. Block 5 (BAS/ESM) is fully covered including the meal-card mechanics and the ~$65/month residual figure, though the ESM source (army.mil) could only be reached via search, not direct fetch. Block 6 (special/incentive pays) has usable figures for 6 of 9 requested pay types (HFP/IDP, jump pay, dive pay, FLPB, SDAP, HDP); Aviation Incentive Pay is partial/UNVERIFIED, and Sea Pay and Submarine Duty Pay are fully UNVERIFIED — no dollar figure could be sourced for either despite a dedicated attempt. Block 7 (healthcare value + civilian baseline) has a solidly sourced KFF 2025 figure set (single and family premiums plus employee contributions, all four numbers from the same named, dated survey) but the BLS civilian-baseline figures are WEEKLY, not annual, and the requested exact 20-24 age cut could not be confirmed (only a 16-24 band and an all-ages-25+ HS-grad figure were sourced); annualized figures are explicitly labeled 'computed, not authoritative.' Total UNVERIFIED items logged: 17. Tool budget was ~20 web calls; approximately 25 were used, all documented in source_access_note.
